---
title: "Notes on The Mixtape: Synthetic Control"
subtitle: "Detailed Study Notes for Chapter 10"
author: "Luke Heley"
date: 5 April 2026
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: false
---

## Purpose

These are study notes on [*Causal Inference: The Mixtape*, Chapter 10 ("Synthetic Control")](https://mixtape.scunning.com/10-synthetic_control). They are meant to support understanding and recall rather than reproduce the chapter. The structure follows the source chapter so the notes can be read alongside it.

The aim here is to capture:

- the main idea of each section
- why the section matters for causal inference
- what conceptual traps to watch for
- how the chapter connects to this repo's teaching materials

## Source Baseline

- Source: [The Mixtape, Chapter 10 - Synthetic Control](https://mixtape.scunning.com/10-synthetic_control)
- Author: Scott Cunningham
- Accessed for these notes: 5 April 2026
- Current chapter sections observed in the live rendering:
  - `10.1 Introducing the Comparative Case Study`
  - `10.1.1 Cuba, Miami, and the Mariel Boatlift`
  - `10.1.2 Picking synthetic controls`
  - `10.1.3 Formalization`
  - `10.1.4 California's Proposition 99`
  - `10.1.5 Falsifications`
  - `10.2 Prison Construction and Black Male Incarceration`
  - `10.3 Conclusion`

## Executive Summary

- The chapter teaches synthetic control as a disciplined version of the comparative case study. Its core move is to replace hand-picked comparators with a transparent donor-weighting procedure that can be inspected, challenged, and diagnosed.
- A recurring theme is that synthetic control does not remove judgment; it relocates judgment into donor-pool design, predictor choice, and falsification work. The optimizer matters, but the credibility of the design still depends on decisions the researcher makes before and around estimation.
- The chapter is strongest when it moves from intuition to workflow. The California tobacco example makes the estimator legible, the inference section explains why placebo-based RMSPE comparisons matter, and the Texas prison example shows what it looks like to replicate a full SCM workflow rather than stop at one plot.
- For this repo, the chapter is most useful as a bridge between the [Synthetic Control overview](synthetic-control.qmd), the [Synthetic Control Sources](synthetic-control-sources.qmd), and the practical SCM labs in `docs/labs/`.

## Chapter Structure and Notes

### 10.1 Introducing the Comparative Case Study

Main idea:

- The chapter introduces synthetic control as an answer to a comparative-case-study problem: how to construct a credible counterfactual for one treated aggregate unit when no single untreated unit looks convincing on its own.

Why this matters:

- This framing keeps the method anchored in causal design rather than in optimization mechanics.
- It clarifies why SCM often appears in settings like states, cities, firms, or countries where treatment happens once and at a high level of aggregation.
- It also shows why SCM is best understood as a refinement of case-comparison logic, not as a generic prediction algorithm.

Key understanding points:

- Comparative case studies often have compelling substantive narratives but weakly specified counterfactuals.
- SCM improves that situation by choosing donor weights explicitly rather than relying on informal control selection.
- The method is attractive when ordinary difference-in-differences feels too coarse for a single treated unit with idiosyncratic dynamics.

What to remember:

- The chapter is not selling SCM as "better regression." It is selling it as a more disciplined way to do small-sample comparative causal work.
- If the counterfactual question is vague, the synthetic control exercise will also be vague.

### 10.1.1 Cuba, Miami, and the Mariel Boatlift

Main idea:

- The Mariel Boatlift example motivates SCM by showing both the appeal and the weakness of classical comparative case studies: Card's design is substantively sharp, but the choice of comparison cities remains partly ad hoc.

Why this matters:

- The example makes clear what SCM is trying to repair.
- It also highlights that uncertainty in comparative case studies is not only sampling uncertainty; it is also uncertainty about whether the chosen controls can reproduce the missing counterfactual.

Key understanding points:

- Card's Miami study is useful because treatment happened sharply and at an aggregate level, making a case-study design natural.
- The weakness is that the four comparison cities were selected by researcher judgment rather than by an explicit weighting rule.
- Cunningham uses this example to show why one weighted synthetic comparator can be more persuasive than a small, hand-picked control set.

What to remember:

- SCM starts from a real applied complaint: "why these controls and not others?"
- This section is a reminder that design transparency is one of SCM's major selling points.

### 10.1.2 Picking synthetic controls

Main idea:

- This section complicates the standard SCM sales pitch by arguing that the method does not eliminate researcher discretion; it changes where that discretion enters.

Why this matters:

- It is one of the most practically useful parts of the chapter because it pushes against the idea that "the machine chose the weights" is enough to guarantee objectivity.
- It also introduces imperfect-fit problems and specification-search risks that appear quickly in real applications.

Key understanding points:

- Researcher choices about predictors, pre-treatment windows, and matching specifications still shape the final result.
- Weak pre-treatment fit is not a small nuisance. It signals either noise, transitory shocks, weak support, or an over-ambitious design.
- The chapter emphasizes that cherry-picking across specifications remains possible unless the analyst is explicit about alternative models and their robustness.

What to remember:

- SCM can make researcher choices more legible, but it does not make them disappear.
- Donor-pool definition and predictor selection are design decisions that deserve the same scrutiny as any regression specification.

### 10.1.3 Formalization

Main idea:

- The formalization section writes SCM as a constrained weighting problem: choose non-negative donor weights that sum to one and minimize distance between treated and donor-weighted predictors before treatment.

Why this matters:

- This is the chapter's clearest statement of what the optimizer is actually doing.
- It ties the intuitive "build a synthetic unit" story to a precise design rule that can be inspected and defended.

Key understanding points:

- The post-treatment treatment effect is the treated outcome minus the weighted donor outcome in each post-treatment period.
- Predictor choice matters because the optimizer is only as good as the balance target it is given.
- The non-negativity and adding-up constraints generate the convex-hull property: classical SCM interpolates within the donor pool rather than extrapolating beyond it.

What to remember:

- No-extrapolation is both a virtue and a constraint.
- If the treated unit lies outside the donor pool's support, poor fit is often telling you something real about the design rather than only something technical about the estimator.

### 10.1.4 California's Proposition 99

Main idea:

- The California tobacco application shows the canonical SCM workflow in action: define a treated unit, exclude contaminated donors, choose predictors and lags, inspect pre-treatment balance, then interpret the post-treatment gap.

Why this matters:

- This is the chapter's main teaching example for how SCM is actually judged in applied work.
- It moves the method from abstract notation to a picture-driven design where pre-treatment fit and donor plausibility do most of the persuasive work.

Key understanding points:

- The balance table matters because SCM still needs a legible comparison between the treated unit and its synthetic analogue, especially on lagged outcomes.
- The path plot and gap plot are central because SCM is a picture-intensive estimator: the core claim is that the synthetic unit tracks the treated unit before treatment and then diverges after it.
- Placebo-based inference uses the post/pre RMSPE ratio to ask whether the treated unit's divergence is unusually large relative to donor-pool placebos.
- Dropping placebo units with much worse pre-treatment fit than the treated unit is presented as a readability and comparability choice, not as a substitute for the exact rank-based \(p\)-value.

What to remember:

- The California example is the chapter's clearest argument that SCM credibility lives in diagnostics, not just in one treatment-effect estimate.
- If you cannot explain the donor pool, the fit, the balance table, and the placebo ranking, you do not yet have a defensible SCM result.

### 10.1.5 Falsifications

Main idea:

- The falsification section adds a second layer of discipline beyond placebo-by-unit inference: rerun the model with an earlier fake treatment date and ask whether the same design spuriously finds an effect.

Why this matters:

- This is the chapter's most direct statement that convincing SCM work requires more than running `synth` once.
- Placebo-date checks test whether the estimated divergence really looks like a treatment-timed structural break rather than a feature of a badly chosen model.

Key understanding points:

- Cunningham uses the German reunification example to illustrate placebo-date falsification rather than to reteach SCM from scratch.
- A good placebo-date exercise asks whether the model behaves sensibly out of sample when treatment is rewound to a period where no effect should exist.
- This complements placebo-by-unit inference: one checks whether the treated unit is unusual relative to donors, the other checks whether the model itself is spuriously unstable over time.

What to remember:

- A strong SCM workflow combines fit, donor transparency, placebo-based exact \(p\)-values, and placebo-date falsification.
- In this repo, this is the clearest bridge from chapter intuition to the more diagnostic-first workflow used in [Synthetic Control overview](synthetic-control.qmd).

### 10.2 Prison Construction and Black Male Incarceration

Main idea:

- The Texas prison example turns the chapter from explanation into replication-oriented practice. The goal is not only to show one more SCM estimate, but to walk the reader through what it looks like to estimate, inspect, and stress-test a full application.

Why this matters:

- This section is where the chapter becomes operational.
- It exposes the reader to a realistic workflow with donor lists, predictor choices, code in multiple languages, saved outputs, placebo loops, and iterative refinement.
- It also shows why SCM is often taught as a craft: the analysis depends on repeated inspection rather than a single one-line estimator.

Key understanding points:

- The substantive setup matters because it motivates why Texas in 1993 can be treated as a comparative case study around prison-capacity expansion.
- Cunningham explicitly treats convex-hull plausibility and pre-treatment resemblance as design assets before estimation, not as afterthoughts.
- The Stata, R, and Python examples are valuable mainly because they reveal workflow: prepare data, run SCM, inspect weights and paths, then build placebo distributions and RMSPE comparisons.
- The closing exercise asks the reader to change the dependent variable and run a placebo-date check, which makes the section a guided replication rather than a finished proof.

What to remember:

- The Texas section is the chapter's best reminder that SCM is learned by iterating through a full design-and-diagnostics sequence.
- For this repo, it pairs naturally with the [Synthetic Control Design Lab](../../labs/synthetic-control-design-lab.qmd) and [Synthetic Control Donor Pool Lab](../../labs/synthetic-control-donor-pool-lab.qmd).

### 10.3 Conclusion

Main idea:

- The conclusion presents the chapter as a foundation: enough formal structure and workflow detail to use SCM responsibly, without pretending the frontier questions around the method are settled.

Why this matters:

- It explains the chapter's balance between intuition, examples, and code.
- It also frames the chapter as an entry point to later methodological developments rather than the final word on synthetic-control research.

Key understanding points:

- Cunningham intentionally keeps the chapter close to canonical SCM rather than trying to cover the full extension literature.
- That makes the chapter especially useful for building a durable baseline before moving on to augmented SCM, synthetic DiD, or other modern variants.

What to remember:

- This chapter is the right first detailed note if you want the core SCM workflow in causal-inference language.
- The next step in this repo is usually to move outward, either to the [Synthetic Control Sources](synthetic-control-sources.qmd) page for follow-on reading or to the SCM labs for practice.

## What This Chapter Adds To This Repo

- It gives the clearest intuition-first account in the repo for why SCM is a comparative-case-study design rather than merely a panel estimator [@cunningham2018synthetic].
- It sharpens the warning that donor-pool design and predictor choice remain researcher decisions, even when donor weights are algorithmically chosen.
- It makes RMSPE-based placebo inference and placebo-date falsification feel central rather than optional, which should inform how the repo's SCM labs and future notes are written.
- It provides the best source-following bridge between the high-level [Synthetic Control overview](synthetic-control.qmd) and the more implementation-facing materials in `docs/labs/`.
